This review will be concerned primarily with thermal
alkali-metal ion association reactions of the general type:
A  M  N  A  M  N. Where A denotes a positively
charged alkali metal ion, M is neutral species and N works as a
third body. As indicated, most association reactions are
reversible, and the A±M bond derives primarily from electro-
static forces whose energy (af®nity) is typically 50 kcal/mol or
less, often much less. The review includes reaction mechanism,
instrumentation, and application to mass spectrometry, together
with reaction rates and alkali ion af®nities of the classi®ed
compounds. ß 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc., Mass Spec Rev
19: 111±138, 2000

 I. INTRODUCTION
Thermal alkali-metal ion association reactions of the
general type are:
A  M  N  A  M  N
1
Where A denotes a positively charged alkali metal ion,
M is neutral species and N works as a third body. As
indicated, most association reactions are reversible,
though not necessarily appreciably so for all experimental
conditions. The bonding between ion and neutral species
is generally found to be weak relative to normal chemical
bond strengths; Do (A±M) is typically 50 kcal/mol or
less, often much less. It seems likely that the A±M bond
derives primarily from electrostatic forces such as ion-
dipole attraction.
Apart from their intrinsic interest as a chemical
process, such association reaction can provide a great deal
of information on the nature of solvation. Equation (1)
may be regarded as representing merely the ®rst step in a
series of reactions leading to the fully solvated ion. It has
also been found that ion-molecule association is a
particularly important process in the ionosphere (Anicich,
1993).
The thermodynamical studies of alkali-ion metal/
molecule reactions began in the mid-sixties. Hogg and
Kebarle reported the gas phase reaction involving the
clustering of water molecules around the potassium ion
in 1965 (Hogg & Kebarle, 1965). They established the
thermodynamics on the solvation of ions (Kebarle et al.,
1967) and this work might be considered as the origin of
alkali-ion metal/molecule reaction studies. The study of
dynamics (reaction rate) is also important in ion-molecule
association. These two different areas of interest can in
practice be investigated by using mass spectrometrical
techniques.
In biological systems alkali metal ions, especially
Na and K, interact with a variety of biologically
important peptides and proteins. The interaction between
organic compounds or biologically active molecules and
alkali-metal cations in the gas phase has attracted much
attention (Dougherty, 1996; Mecozzi, West Jr, & Dough-
erty, 1996). Such interactions are important in chemical
and biological processes occurring in solution; for
example, ion solvation, catalysis, transport through
membranes, af®nity of active compounds toward receptor,
and antibiotic activity. A detailed knowledge of the
geometry and free energy of ion complexation in the gas
phase is required before attempting condensed-phase
modeling by theoretical techniques.
The present article is intended to cover both ®elds.
The literature has been surveyed up to the end of 1997,
although some more recent works have also been
included. Thermochemistry has been discussed in early
reviews by Kebarle (1977) covering the period up through
1976. Several others devoted largely to the authors' own
works (Beauchamp, 1971; Anu & Bowers, 1979; Taft,
1983; Lias, Liebman, & Levin, 1984; Castleman Jr &
Keesee, 1986; Graul & Squires, 1988; Cooks et al., 1994).
Other general reviews (Franklin & Harland, 1974; Good,
1975) contain some information related to the reaction
kinetics.
This review (i) explores the mechanism of alkali-
metal ion/molecule association reaction; (ii) surveys the
instrumentational basis to study its kinetics; (iii) describes
the instrumentation to the measurement of alkali-metal
ion af®nities, covering recent theoretical studies that
re®ne the experimental results, and (iv) summarizes
applications of this reaction to measurements on chemical
and biological compounds in the mass spectrometry ®eld.
The subject of transition-metal ion reactions is not
covered.
II. MECHANISM AND REACTION RATE
A. Mechanism of Association Reaction
Ion association reactions are described as the general
form (Good, 1975):
A  M  A  M
combination; k1=decomposition; k2
2
A  M  N  A  M  N
stabilization; k3=reverse; k4 3
Where A denotes a positively charged ion, M and N are
neutral species and k1, k2, k3 and k4 are rate constants. It
has been pointed out that an ion-molecule association
reaction is a process very similar to the combination of
two atoms or two free radicals. An initial combination
step proceeds without activation energy by ion-permanent
dipole or ion-induced dipole attraction between the
reactants. The energy thus accumulated in the reaction
complex could then be liberated by collision with a third
body, N. As indicated, most association reactions are
reversible, though not necessarily appreciably so for all
experimental conditions.
B. Termolecular Reaction Kinetics
The basic kinetics of termolecular association is usually
approximated by a two-step mechanism (Spears &
Ferguson, 1973; Franklin & Harland, 1974; Good,
1975). For the association of ion to a molecule M with
